commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Manual maintainer handling is needed because the remaining blocker is proof acceptance or real unsupported-mount proof, not a narrow code defect for automation to repair.; Cleared: The diff adds no dependencies, workflows, package metadata, permissions, secrets handling, or supply-chain execution surface. (sha=70d88cf324054bee1b83ab39f6fe696142538223)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds an O_EXCL lock-file fallback for session usage-cost refresh lock creation when hard links are unsupported and adds a regression test for `ENOTSUP`.

PR surface: Source +17, Tests +27. Total +44 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main unconditionally uses `fs.promises.link` for lock publication and rethrows non-`EEXIST` errors, matching the linked `ENOTSUP` failure path. I did not establish a live unsupported-mount reproduction.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Manual maintainer handling is needed because the remaining blocker is proof acceptance or real unsupported-mount proof, not a narrow code defect for automation to repair.

target.title: fix(session-cost-usage): fall back to O_EXCL write when fs.link is unsupported (#81089)
target.body:
## Summary

`writeUsageCostCacheLockAtomically` in `src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts` publishes the usage-cost cache refresh lock by `fs.promises.link`'ing a fully-formed temp file to `<cache>.lock`. On filesystems without hard-link support — SMB/CIFS, some NFS setups, virtiofs/9p shared folders, certain FUSE stacks — `link(2)` rejects with `ENOTSUP` (often surfaced as "ENOTSUP: operation not supported on socket"). The catch in `acquireUsageCostCacheRefreshLock` only handles `EEXIST`, so this `ENOTSUP` propagates as an unhandled crash and the refresh path dies.

Fixes #81089.

## Fix

Catch the small known-set of "hard-link unavailable" `errno`s (`ENOTSUP`, `EPERM`, `EXDEV`, `EOPNOTSUPP`) on `fs.promises.link`, and fall back to writing the lock payload directly with `flag: "wx"` (O_EXCL create-only). This preserves the same `EEXIST` contention semantics — concurrent writers on the same filesystem still race for atomic exclusive creation — while making lock acquisition portable across the failing mount types listed in the issue.

`EEXIST` continues to propagate to the existing `acquireUsageCostCacheRefreshLock` catch (busy-or-stale handling unchanged). Cross-filesystem race semantics between hard-link and O_EXCL are identical for this use case because the lock payload is a single fully-formed JSON object written in one step.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `refreshCostUsageCache({ agentId: "main" })` crashes with `ENOTSUP: operation not supported on socket, link …` on filesystems where `link(2)` rejects (SMB/CIFS, some NFS setups, virtiofs/9p shared folders, certain FUSE stacks — see issue #81089 for the reporter's mount). The unhandled rejection kills the refresh path, leaving usage-cost stale.
- Real environment tested: local dev shell on darwin/arm64 against the real Node 22.21.1 `node:fs` runtime, with `fs.promises.link` monkey-patched at module level to reject with the issue's verbatim errno (`code: "ENOTSUP"`, message prefix `ENOTSUP: operation not supported on socket, link`) so the fallback path under `writeUsageCostCacheLockAtomically` is exercised against actual on-disk reads/writes, not against a Vitest spy.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```
  node /tmp/proof-81388.mjs
  ```
  where `proof-81388.mjs` mkdir's a real temp `<cache>/agents/main/sessions/` tree under `os.tmpdir()`, monkey-patches `fs.promises.link` to reject with the issue's exact `ENOTSUP` errno, runs the patched `writeUsageCostCacheLockAtomically` body inline (byte-for-byte from `src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts:236-256`), stats the resulting lock file, then re-runs the same write to confirm the second attempt rejects with `EEXIST` (same O_EXCL contention semantics as the original link-based publish).
- Evidence after fix:
  ```
  $ node /tmp/proof-81388.mjs
  tmp: /var/folders/xb/m960rmw13v7g9qmb3ssqzhyh0000gq/T/openclaw-81388-proof-0FDEkA
  lock written ok via fallback (link rejected ENOTSUP)
  lock file mode: 0644
  lock file bytes: 61
  lock file payload: {"pid":11489,"startedAt":1778759440802,"token":"11489:demo"}
  second writer code: EEXIST
  ```
- Observed result after fix: with `node` exercising the patched body against the real darwin/arm64 filesystem, the fallback branch (a) catches the `ENOTSUP` from `fs.promises.link`, (b) re-publishes the lock payload via `fs.promises.writeFile(lockPath, payload, { flag: "wx" })` so the on-disk lock file lands with the same bytes a successful `link` would have produced (61-byte JSON-line payload, mode 0644), and (c) a second concurrent writer hits `EEXIST` from O_EXCL — proving the contention semantics that `acquireUsageCostCacheRefreshLock`'s existing `EEXIST` catch already handles are preserved end-to-end across the fallback. Before this patch, the equivalent driver crashes with the un-handled `ENOTSUP` from `link` and never reaches the lock-file-on-disk state.
- What was not tested: an actual remote SMB/CIFS, NFS, virtiofs, or 9p mount in this contributor's local environment; the four eligible errno codes (`ENOTSUP`, `EPERM`, `EXDEV`, `EOPNOTSUPP`) were collapsed to one path in this driver since the fallback branch is identical across all of them.

## Notes

Scope is intentionally narrow: only the `writeUsageCostCacheLockAtomically` call site. No other `fs.promises.link` usage was touched. The set of fallback-eligible errno codes is named with a single `Set` so it can be expanded if additional `link`-rejection surfaces are observed in the wild without re-shaping the rest of the function.

Tested against `node:fs` semantics on Node 22.21.1 / darwin arm64; no Node version gating needed (`writeFile` `flag: "wx"` is supported across all Node ≥12).
target.author: 0xghost42

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge

commands.author: 0xghost42
commands.command: acknowledged on the real-environment gap — wanted to be transparent about why the pr body's proof stops where it does. this contributor's local environment is darwin/arm64 without admin / sudo, which blocks the three obvious shapes of "real unsupported-hardlink mount": - `hdiutil create -fs exfat` fails with `operation not permitted` (system integrity protection blocks disk-image creation in this context). exfat would be the closest cheap match — on darwin, exfat rejects `link(2)` with `eopnotsupp`, which is one of the four codes the fallback's `hard_link_unsupported_codes` set covers. - `mount_smbfs` requires a reachable smb server (the reporter's mount class). not available locally. - fuse-based unsupported-link filesystems (9p / virtiofs / custom fuse) need `sudo mount` permissions. so the proof in the pr body intercepts at the same syscall boundary the real-mount error would surface from — `fs.promises.link` rejecting with `code: "enotsup"`, message prefix `enotsup: operation not supported on socket, link …` (verbatim from issue #81089's stack trace). the downstream `writefile(lockpath, payload, { flag: "wx" })` is real on-disk i/o against the actual darwin/arm64 `node:fs` runtime — apfs rather than the affected mount, but the contention semantics (`eexist` on second writer) are o_excl-level not filesystem-specific. so the only thing the monkey-patch substitutes for is the link-call rejection itself, which is exactly what the reporter's mount produces. two ways forward, picking whichever the team prefers: 1. **accept the existing proof under the maintainer-skip clause** in `agents.md` ("maintainers: may skip/ignore real behavior proof when local tests or crabbox verified behavior; record proof in pr verification."). the code shape was already accepted by the review summary ("the proposed code shape is the narrow maintainable fix"); only the proof origin is in question. 2. **run via crabbox / testbox with a real smb or 9p mount** for a true end-to-end. happy to update the pr body with the resulting log when a maintainer can trigger it — i cannot kick off crabbox myself. no further code push from my side until you say which path; the existing fix already matches the bot's accepted shape and the monkey-patch driver is the strongest reproducible proof under contributor control here.
commands.freeform_prompt: acknowledged on the real-environment gap — wanted to be transparent about why the PR body's proof stops where it does. This contributor's local environment is darwin/arm64 without admin / sudo, which blocks the three obvious shapes of "real unsupported-hardlink mount": - `hdiutil create -fs ExFAT` fails with `Operation not permitted` (System Integrity Protection blocks disk-image creation in this context). exFAT would be the closest cheap match — on darwin, exFAT rejects `link(2)` with `EOPNOTSUPP`, which is one of the four codes the fallback's `HARD_LINK_UNSUPPORTED_CODES` set covers. - `mount_smbfs` requires a reachable SMB server (the reporter's mount class). Not available locally. - FUSE-based unsupported-link filesystems (9p / virtiofs / custom FUSE) need `sudo mount` permissions. So the proof in the PR body intercepts at the same syscall boundary the real-mount error would surface from — `fs.promises.link` rejecting with `code: "ENOTSUP"`, message prefix `ENOTSUP: operation not supported on socket, link …` (verbatim from issue #81089's stack trace). The downstream `writeFile(lockPath, payload, { flag: "wx" })` is real on-disk I/O against the actual darwin/arm64 `node:fs` runtime — APFS rather than the affected mount, but the contention semantics (`EEXIST` on second writer) are O_EXCL-level not filesystem-specific. So the only thing the monkey-patch substitutes for is the link-call rejection itself, which is exactly what the reporter's mount produces. Two ways forward, picking whichever the team prefers: 1. **Accept the existing proof under the maintainer-skip clause** in `AGENTS.md` ("Maintainers: may skip/ignore Real behavior proof when local tests or Crabbox verified behavior; record proof in PR verification."). The code shape was already accepted by the review summary ("the proposed code shape is the narrow maintainable fix"); only the proof origin is in question. 2. **Run via Crabbox / Testbox with a real SMB or 9p mount** for a true end-to-end. Happy to update the PR body with the resulting log when a maintainer can trigger it — I cannot kick off Crabbox myself. No further code push from my side until you say which path; the existing fix already matches the bot's accepted shape and the monkey-patch driver is the strongest reproducible proof under contributor control here.
